title: Lists
subtitle: Keep a whole bunch of things together in one tidy backpack.
goal: Make lists, grab items with indexing and slicing, change them, add and remove things, and use handy tools like len(), sort(), min(), max(), and sum().

blocks.heading: What is a list? 🎒
blocks.html:
Imagine a shopping list, or your backpack full of stuff. Instead of having a different box for every single thing, you keep them all together in one place, in order.

In Python, that's a list. It can hold names, numbers, words — anything — all lined up one after another. 🥳

blocks.heading: Making a list with [ ]
blocks.html: You build a list with square brackets [ ] and put commas between the items. Let's make a list of friends and print it.

blocks.caption: A list of friends
blocks.explain: The square brackets [ ] make a list. len() counts how many items are inside — here that's 3.

blocks.html: Order counts! A list remembers the order you put things in. The first item stays first until YOU move it.

blocks.heading: Grabbing items: indexing 🔢
blocks.html:
Just like the letters in a word (remember Day 3?), each spot in a list has a number called an index. And Python starts counting at 0, not 1!

So in ["Mia", "Leo", "Zoe"], Mia is at 0, Leo at 1, and Zoe at 2. You can also count backwards: -1 is the last item.

blocks.caption: Pick items by their position
blocks.explain: scores[0] is the first item. scores[-1] is the last. scores[1:3] is a slice — it grabs from index 1 up to (but not including) 3.

blocks.html: Watch out! Counting starts at 0. So the 1st item is index 0 and the 3rd item is index 2. This trips up everyone at first — you'll get used to it. 😄

blocks.heading: Changing, adding, and removing 🛠️
blocks.html:
Lists are like a whiteboard — you can rub things out and write new ones. You can swap an item, add new ones, or take some away.

  • .append(x) — add x to the end
  • .insert(i, x) — slot x in at position i
  • .remove(x) — take out the first x
  • .pop() — remove the last item (and hand it back to you)

blocks.caption: A to-do list that changes
blocks.code:
todo = ["brush teeth", "feed cat", "homework"]
todo.append("play outside")
todo.insert(0, "wake up")
todo.remove("homework")
done = todo.pop()
print("Still to do:", todo)
print("Just finished:", done)
blocks.explain: append adds to the end, insert(0, ...) slots in at the front, remove deletes by name, and pop() takes off the last item and gives it back.

blocks.heading: Is it in there? Using in ✅
blocks.html: Want to check if something is in your list? The word in gives you back True or False.

blocks.caption: Checking membership and using number tools
blocks.code:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "mango"]
print("mango" in fruits)
print("grape" in fruits)

scores = [40, 10, 55, 25]
print("Lowest:", min(scores))
print("Highest:", max(scores))
print("Total:", sum(scores))
blocks.explain: "mango" in fruits is True because it's there. min(), max(), and sum() work on lists of numbers to find the smallest, biggest, and total.

blocks.heading: Putting things in order 📋
blocks.html:
Two ways to sort:

  • .sort() rearranges the list itself, for keeps.
  • sorted(mylist) hands you a new sorted list and leaves the original alone.

blocks.caption: Sorting two different ways
blocks.code:
nums = [4, 1, 7, 2]
print("Sorted copy:", sorted(nums))
print("Original still:", nums)

nums.sort()
print("After .sort():", nums)

names = ["Zoe", "Mia", "Leo"]
names.sort()
for name in names:
    print(name)
blocks.explain: sorted() makes a new sorted list without changing the original. .sort() changes the list for real. The little for loop just prints each name on its own line.

blocks.question: What is the index of the FIRST item in a list?
blocks.explain: Python starts counting at 0, so the first item is always at index 0.

blocks.question: Which one adds an item to the END of a list?
blocks.explain: append() sticks a new item onto the end. insert(0, x) would add it to the front, and pop()/remove() take things away.

blocks.question: What does sum([1, 2, 3]) give you?
blocks.explain: sum() adds up all the numbers in the list: 1 + 2 + 3 = 6.

blocks.title: Build your friends list
blocks.instructions: Make a list called friends with at least 3 names inside the [ ] brackets. Then print the list AND print how many friends there are using len(). Press Check.
blocks.starter:
friends = ["___", "___", "___"]
print(friends)
# print how many friends with len()
blocks.passMsg: Nice — your backpack of friends is ready! 🎒
blocks.failMsg: Make sure you have a list in [ ] brackets, print it, and use len() to count.

blocks.title: Add, then find the top score
blocks.instructions: A scores list is started for you. Use .append() to add the score 90, then print the biggest score using max(). Press Check.
blocks.starter:
scores = [70, 55, 80]
# append 90 to the list
# print the biggest score with max()
blocks.passMsg: 90 is the new high score — well played! 🏅
blocks.failMsg: Use scores.append(90) to add it, then print(max(scores)) to show the highest.

challenge.title: The party guest list 🎉
challenge.instructions: Start with a guest list of 3 names. Then: 1) use .append() to add a new guest, 2) use .sort() to put the names in alphabetical order, 3) print the final sorted list, and 4) print how many guests are coming using len().
challenge.starter:
guests = ["Zoe", "Mia", "Leo"]
# 1) append one more guest
# 2) sort the list
# 3) print the sorted list
# 4) print the number of guests with len()
challenge.solution:
guests = ["Zoe", "Mia", "Leo"]
guests.append("Ava")
guests.sort()
print(guests)
print("Guests coming:", len(guests))
challenge.hint: Do them in order: guests.append("..."), then guests.sort(), then print(guests), then print(len(guests)).
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Party planned and sorted! You've finished Day 8 — lists are officially yours.
